does: This paper introduces RoboAlign-R1, a framework that improves robot video world models by aligning them with task-relevant objectives through reward-based post-training. The authors create RobotWorldBench, a benchmark of 10,000 annotated video-instruction pairs from four robot datasets, and develop RoboAlign-Judge, a multimodal teacher model that evaluates generated videos across six dimensions including instruction following, manipulation success, and physical plausibility. They distill this teacher into a lightweight student reward model for efficient reinforcement-learning-based post-training of world models. Additionally, they propose Sliding Window Re-encoding (SWR), a training-free inference technique that periodically refreshes the generation context to mitigate error accumulation during long-horizon autoregressive prediction.
connects: This work builds on the established line of research in robot learning that recognizes the gap between low-level training objectives (reconstruction, perceptual similarity) and high-level task performance. It extends prior work on video world models and behavioral cloning for robotics by incorporating reward learning and alignment techniques developed in the broader reinforcement learning community. The approach combines insights from recent work on distillation in generative models with multimodal evaluation frameworks, addressing known challenges in autoregressive video prediction such as compounding errors. The construction of a large-scale annotated benchmark connects to efforts in other domains to create evaluation standards for generative models aligned with downstream task performance.
enables: This framework opens pathways for developing more capable robot world models that can serve as better planning and decision-making tools. The RobotWorldBench benchmark and evaluation methodology enable future research in reward alignment for robotics beyond video models. The techniques could extend to other embodied AI domains requiring long-horizon generation, and the distillation approach provides a template for scaling multimodal evaluation to more efficient inference. By demonstrating how to align generative models with robot-specific capabilities, this work enables more sample-efficient robot learning systems that can leverage world models for model-based control, planning, and sim-to-real transfer while maintaining fidelity over extended prediction horizons.
